narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background, you've put real effort into your path toward law school, including choosing full-time studies ahead. Our admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated an 84% chance you'll face failure on that crucial test—far higher than what we'd see in approved applicants. This high-risk prediction led to the tough decision to reject your application, as the school prioritizes those with stronger signals of bar passage to maintain our outcomes. The biggest factor pulling toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which stands out well below the typical 5.5-ish average and signaled weaker late-stage academic momentum compared to most students who pass. Right behind that, your LSAT score of 20—much lower than the average around 37—strongly reinforced the model's concerns, as it's a key hurdle where many successful bar takers excel far higher. On a brighter note, your Year 1 decile of 5 was right around average and nudged things slightly toward success, showing early promise that helped balance the scales a bit. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 2.7, a touch below the 3.2 average, offered some counterweight but wasn't enough to offset the top issues. Your middle family income and full-time commitment played minor roles, barely tipping the needle either way in line with most applicants. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having average early performance and background but lagging significantly in those critical later grades and LSAT, where standouts usually shine to predict bar success. While this stings, it's not the end—consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score dramatically, strengthening your Year 3 record through additional coursework, and reapplying with those upgrades to shift your predicted odds. We're rooting for your growth, and many have turned similar profiles around with targeted improvements. Keep pushing forward—you've got this potential.
